transcription:
LC algorithm:

1) Every process will keep a counter, initially 0.

2) On every event, a process increments it's counter by 1.

3) When sending a message, a process includes
its counter with the message.

4) When receiving a message, set your
clock to [max( local clock, received clock )+1]

- The Lamport clock does not guarentee
a happens before relation (look at nodes E&G)
↓        (inverse clock condition)
If LC(A) < LC(B), this doesn't mean A→B.
It can also mean ~~A # B~~.

~~e₁ # e₂~~ e₁→e₂ ⟹ LC(e₁) < LC(e₂)
            contrapositive:
   LC(e₁) ≥ LC(e₂) ⟹ ~(e₂→e₁) V (e₁ || e₂)

Vector Clocks  guarentee inverse clock condition

1) Every process maintains a vector of natural numbers,
initialized to 0. The length of the vector is the
number of processes.

2) On every event, a process increments its own
position ~~num~~ in the clock

blocks.text: 1. Every process will keep a counter, initially 0.
2. On every event, a process increments it's counter by 1.
3. When sending a message, a process includes its counter with the message.
4. When receiving a message, set your clock to $[\max(\text{local clock}, \text{received clock})+1]$

items.text: The Lamport clock does not guarentee a happens before relation (look at nodes E&G) (inverse clock condition)
If $LC(A) < LC(B)$, this doesn't mean $A \to B$.

blocks.text: Vector Clocks guarentee inverse clock condition
1. Every process maintains a vector of natural numbers, initialized to 0. The length of the vector is the number of processes.
2. On every event, a process increments its own position in the clock
